content:
I had a question earlier about probability theory and logic. There didn't seem to be an appropriate stream - I don't know enough about #theory: topos theory to know if it should be there, it was too specific for #general, didn't seem quite right for #theory: category theory or #practice: applied ct . I posted in #learning: basic questions, but was rightly called out for that, since the question is not very beginner level. The resolution was that a new #theory: logic stream was created and I moved my post there, which is a fine place for it.

But I wonder whether this shows a need for some kind of catch-all stream, other than #general or #learning: basic questions . I don't know whether "advanced questions" is a good name or not, but the idea is just to have a place where we can post technical questions / discussions that don't belong in basic questions but also don't have an obvious 'theory' or 'practice' stream to go in.

I suggest this partly because I think there are some disadvantages to coarse-graining things too finely by topic - just as a personal preference I would prefer to have fewer streams, just because it would make navigating a bit easier. Having a good general-purpose stream for technical discussions would remove some of the need to create new streams for potentially niche topics. (I'm not saying that's the case for #theory: logic necessarily, but it does have quite a bit of overlap with #theory: topos theory and #theory: type theory, which may or may not mean it ends up being a bit low-traffic in practice.)

tl;dr I think a stream along the lines of "advanced questions" or "mathematics: other" might be a useful addition.

The downside of this is that then we'd have many different questions stream with no clear demarcation between them since assessing something as easy or difficult is often a matter of personal judgement. It would make navigation more difficult indeed
Probably renaming #learning: basic questions to something a bit more general could do the trick?

That's true also. (Coming from another field I often have trouble knowing whether my questions are beginner level or not.) It's definitely good to have a place for beginner questions, but maybe an additional catch-all stream should be more along the lines of "other mathematical topics" than "advanced questions."

Anyway I expect there problems to be at least partially solved as soon as we can move topics to other streams. Many of these problems can be solved by moving a conversation elsewhere, unfortunately we have to do it manually atm but this is going to change eventually

That will solve some of it for sure. In this case the issue was that a new stream needed to be created just because there wasn't an existing one that perfectly fit the topic - I was mostly thinking about how to solve that issue, so that we don't end up creating more new streams than necessary.

Nathaniel Virgo said:

I suggest this partly because I think there are some disadvantages to coarse-graining things too finely by topic - just as a personal preference I would prefer to have fewer streams, just because it would make navigating a bit easier. Having a good general-purpose stream for technical discussions would remove some of the need to create new streams for potentially niche topics. (I'm not saying that's the case for #theory: logic necessarily, but it does have quite a bit of overlap with #theory: topos theory and #theory: type theory, which may or may not mean it ends up being a bit low-traffic in practice.)

tl;dr I think a stream along the lines of "advanced questions" or "mathematics: other" might be a useful additionquote
I think "advanced" is rather vague and also a bit intimidating - one person's "advanced" question could be someone's else's "oh, I learned about that in grad school".
I prefer dividing math up into topics. I agree that a coarse-grained classification is better than a fine-grained one.
I came here for all the Games: game theory, game semantics, etc. Shall there be a "theory: games" stream?
Sounds good to me!
Let the games begin!
@Faré: despite game theory and game semantics having "game" in the name, they are very different subjects, no?
in which case it would be better to have separate streams for them each
Yes, they are very different, but they have a non-empty intersection.
The beauty of mathematics is that that's generally true for any two given subjects :grinning_face_with_smiling_eyes:
In this case, I admit I'm interested in the intersection.
Perhaps a theory: game semantics stream would be appropriate — I imagine there are others with an interest in this field, but perhaps fewer with an interest specifically in the intersection?
OK, created https://categorytheory.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/233273-theory.3A-game.20semantics
FYI, you can type #**theory: game semantics** to link to a stream, like this: #theory: game semantics. If you start typing #theory: g, you can autocomplete.
